item.title: Why do workers/poor hate investing? — 70 replies — 111.4/hr
item.content:encoded:
Why do workers/poor hate investing?

Open thread • Replies: 70 • Updated: 2026-05-31 21:30 UTC

OP

Most conversation with friends/family/coworkers I have when it comes to investing long term in Roth IRA, high yield savings, and my ETF Dividend portfolio even with all the money I make working cyber security utilizing FIRE method they get so aggressive about “what if” scenarios thinking I’m day-trading and even when I explain it they don’t get it.

“WHAT IF THE MARKET ALL GOES UNDER AND YOU LOSE EVERYTHING”

“WHAT IF YOU TAKE ALL YOUR MONEY OUT AND SELL ON A LOSS”

“WHAT IF SOMETHING BAD HAPPENS AND YOU HAVE NO MONEY”

Which I retort what happens when you lose your job? When the market goes under and suddenly you’re up for layoffs?

“WELL THAT WILL NEVER HAPPEN BECAUSE IM VALUABLE AND MY SKILLS AND MY TIME AR COMPANY AND-“

Like do these people really think working for someone for the rest of your life is the only way to live? I’d rather live frugally but free, and that really seems to irk people.


What the hell is this psychology? Why do they hate the idea someone doesn’t wanna work for someone for the rest of their life or majority? Do we forget money is to buy our freedom?

Early replies (first 3)

62294872
They are physically, mentally incapable of visualizing what you are trying to explain in terms of long term time horizons, compounding returns, etc. It's not worth bothering with. Like trying to talk to a dog.

62294877
There's a reason poor "people" are poor
The final redpill and the toughest to swallow is that jews are 100% right about the goyim

62294881
>>62294870
You probably come across as obnoxious and irritating and you're clearly in your early 20s and lack the maturity to keep shit to yourself. People don't just randomly get aggressive the way you've described, you're missing the key details and you're probably rubbing people up the wrong way and saying all the wrong this because you lack tact and grace.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62295158
>>62294980
>seething this hard
You lost and lost hard, you eternal child. Go cry someplace that cares.

62295173
>>62295155
I recently saw a YouTube video where a guy showed what it would look like if you invested $1,000 every month into the S&P500 if you bought at the absolute worst time (highest price) for each month for seven years. It still made a ton of money. Even if you had the worst luck in human history and chose the worst day to invest every single time you still come out ahead.

62295177
>>62294881

>Bro i know you barely make ends meet already, but if you put 90% of your income into #FARTPEPE you'll be rich bro! I made a killing on it using mommy's creditcard that one time. Why are you so angry bro, don't you want to be a smart investor like me? Why do you hate investing? Why are you subhuman worker trash incapable of understanding simple financial advice? I'm just trying to help!

62295192
>>62295155
And when the price is down, like in 2015 where gold dropped down to $1,000 after a peak around $1,700, it’s "I’m not buying this shit lol". Whatever happens, there’s always a good excuse to do nothing.

62295196
>>62295173
>worst luck in human history
>in the longest and biggest bull market ever

62295198
>>62295173
Yeah, it's the simple saying of "Time in the market beats timing the market".
I always knew I'd be investing from the time I actually earned enough money (which wasn't until I turned 29) until I either get enough for a nice House or I turn 45. Neither has happened yet, but I sleep like a baby while watching the money grow.

62295203
>>62294870
because normalfags don't care about the mathematical trick of turning 10k into a million in 60 years

62295207
>>62294889
normalfags only talk about pussy and cock
you never touch grass

62295211
>>62295198
>Time in the market beats timing the market
meaningless bullshit unless you keep it there at least 20 years
even a 10 year period isn't enough for your meme to protect you against crashes like 2000 and 2008

62295212
>>62294870
i dont talk to people about investing. everyone knows about the stock market. some choose to participate... some choose not to.

they thought about it and dont want to invest in stocks.

62295213
>>62294980
You BTFO'd that samefagging tranny. Good job, anon. Bravo.

62295216
>>62294870
you'll understand when the stock market has a real crash, there's a reason there's lots of laws to protect pleb investors. How does losing 80% of your money and then crabbing for 30 years sound?

62295221
>>62294870
>>62294872

For a lot of people it's a mindset that many never actually obtain. Many people have a high time preference thay revolves around instant gratification. They get their monthly pay and spaff it on frivolities and consumables like drugs, alcohol or funko pop tier BS. People with low time preference understand the need for investments and savings knowing that they will benefit down the line from dividends or having a back up fund when the income dries up.
Some people learn the hard way but many simply don't learn, particularly those in welfare states.

62295226
>>62295173
>DCA for 7 years
And wait for decades to outlast any crash that might occur which basically means you're sacrificing your youth to become a wealthy geriatric. Good idea if you want to afford those Gucci diapers which are very comfy for old scrotums.

62295261
>>62295196
Unironically yes. Good luck would have meant doing the exact same thing from 2000-2012.

62295297
>>62294870
Because their news feed is filled with doomer bears who want them to fail

62295303
>>62294870
>What the hell is this psychology?
Risk aversion, which gets worse the poorer you are.

62295361
>>62294877

62295385
When the average normie has an unexpected windfall of money, the first thing that pops into their head is "what should I spend this money on?". It's like a reflex that has been embedded into them since childhood. They've been programmed by an endless stream of marketing campaigns at every stage of their life to become the ultimate consumers.

Many people are eventually able to break out of this mindset and start to invest long term, but it's usually a process that lasts several years. One or two conversations is not enough to undo a lifetime of programming

62295393
>>62295361
*takes and puts on*

62295428
>>62295385
I remember talking to an attorney who’s firm would advise a lot of lottery winners. He said it took on average 3-5 years for the lottery winners to be able to change their mindset from "spend money as fast as I can" to "I need to protect my money". Which is why like 70% of all lottery winners declare bankruptcy within 5 years. They’re not able to change their mindset fast enough and will instinctively try to spend it all

>>62295211
>bro, you need to hold AT LEAST for 40 years!
Bitchass mentality right there.
Do you even invest, or are you just trading?

>but you gave some to X why aren't you sharing with meeeee why are you so meeeeean
And your parents/kids will say
>don't bogart that stash you should share your good fortune with X, Y, and Z and all their relatives as well
Which is why you should never ever let anybody know you've come into a windfall. Just move away and drop contact before enjoying your winnings.

item.title: How is it that everyone on reddit are millionaires — 10 replies — 40.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
How is it that everyone on reddit are millionaires

Open thread • Replies: 10 • Updated: 2026-05-31 22:05 UTC

OP

>excluding crypto
Seriously, do you all have more than 200k in your brokerage accounts? Its crazy to me that you Americans are that well off. Even on ticktok live streams, you see this guy presenting his 200k+ portfolio in his mid to late 20s. Others on reddit are showcasing their portfolio at mid 30 already reaching 1M+. Yet people all around me are claiming that life has gotten increasingly harder and more expensive. Here is Switzerland we are getting drained with increasing Krankenkasse (health care) costs and mandatory radio-service billings (Serafe) to the point that we are voting to decrease these this month, somehow.
This cant all be fake personal marketing and fake boasting, can it?
Statistics show that the median savings in top wealth western nations is like 50-80k (checking, savings and investments, excluding house, cars etc.) in total.
ALSO...WHERE IS ALL THIS MONEY COMING FROM TO INVEST IN THE STOCK MARKET? How are these companies being pumped so heavily with cash that giga-corps like Micron reach 1 trillion easily overnight?

Early replies (first 3)

62295606
>>62295508
It's called consolidation. There's very few websites of any note and only a few kooks in the developed world are consciously not online so if you're rich you're gonna be in one or two communities.

62295628
>>62295508
??? it's a bull market

62295633
>>62295508
>obsessed with americans backwoods eurohillbilly
lol

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62295651
people with high balances are more willing to share them. so there is selection bias. that said, of course many people in the US will have a higher balance. even though there are people in a ton of debt there is also an investing culture that doesn’t exist as much in Europe. my main point of reference is Germany which has a strong saving culture but not an investing one.

62295737
>>62295508
you're called europoor for a reason you retarded thirdie

62296149
Hint hint: circular trading aka painting the tape. Assets trading back and forth every second between coordinating accounts on carribbean/chinese nodes results in no stock accumulation...only the march up in price occurs. The real accumulation was done years ago during the bear market and sits on the accounts of the top 100 financial institutions. Now is the mark up phase. 100 to 1,000 shares trading every second between 10 circular trading nodes get the job done. The bid and ask are relentlessly marched up. And 401ks and retail provide constant exit liquidity near major tops. These illegal nodes of circular trading don't even have to do most of the heavy lifting and regulators have little case against them. Retail, 401k's, and algorithmic trading does most of the idiotic pumping and fomo buying into overvalued nonsense. And the big wall street routing firms and market makers know what they're looking for to coordinate their trades in coordination with the illegal market manipulating nodes. When you have the world's reserve currency, you must get everything to flow back to you. And our SEC looks away when US stocks are pumped to the moon. Our own con artists make the majority of the money. They figure the money flows back in a small way to the American peasants. It does, but the volatility and resulting inflation never benefits the bottom 75%. And these nodes literally require peanut size capital. They are using a few million dollars to pump the price because they never hold for more than a second. They are constantly pumping both the bid and the ask price by a penny. And they execute on every all time high. Billions and trillions back at the legitimate Wall St firms is growing by 20% a year for 5 years during these bull runs. But the party must end. It is collectively decided upon when to reverse course. They take the short side and then do the opposite. They'll make money on the way down. By coordinating bull runs and occasional bear raids, the SEC goes to sleep

62296160
200k is not that much if you're a single man with a decent job and you don't waste money you can easily build a 200k portfolio in 3 years even if you're just DCA into indices.

62296173
Nobody flexing their $20000 poorfolio. Also yeah dummy, the FIRE subreddit (and other financial subreddits) tend to have more monetarily successful people

62296180
>>62295508
Also bro you could have googled this in like 5 seconds. Retard

62296281
>>62295508
they live with their parents, meaning their parents are millionaires, not them

or their parents passed on some of their wealth to them. There's a lot of Gen X and boomer millionaires here since all you had to do was buy a house in a moderately popular part of the US and you're probably now a millionaire.

or the people on reddit are much older than you expect. For example I'm a millionaire and have been for 20+ years now, but I am also almost 60 years old now. People on 4chan assume I must be lying about money because they can't imagine a 60 year old boomer posting on 4chan.

item.content:encoded:
Honestly owning rentals made me realize how helpless alot of adults are now.

Open thread • Replies: 65 • Updated: 2026-05-31 20:20 UTC

OP

Honestly owning rentals made me realize how helpless alot of adults are now.

I bought a bunch of duplexes down south back around 2018-2020 because the numbers made sense. Cheap property taxes, cheap insurance, cheap properties in general. One duplex I paid $124k for is bringing in around $1925/mo total now and the tennants renewed instantly after the last increase.

Had a tennant last week who submitted a maintenence request because the microwave “stopped heating.” Property manager drove over there and the door wasnt shut all the way.

Another guy called because his screen door came off the track. PM slid it back in place in about 10 seconds and the guy goes “much obliged sir” like he just repaired farm equipment from the 1800s or something.

Whats weird is the older tennants almost never bother anybody. Pay on time. Maybe ask for a sink fix every few years. Younger ones send paragraphs over tiny stuff.

Not even trying to sound mean but I geniunely think people are less capable now than they were 20 years ago.

People online act like owning rentals is some impossible genius level thing but honestly half the job is just dealing with grown adults who dont know why the wifi stopped working after they unplugged the router to move furniture around.

62292634
>>62283816
The funny thing about landlord stories like this is that they're built around a kind of selection bias.

You don't remember the hundreds of tenants who quietly handle small problems themselves. You remember the one who submitted a maintenance ticket because the microwave door wasn't fully closed. That story sticks because it's unusual, not because it's representative.

And honestly, people tend to overestimate how much a single anecdote proves. Someone making a mistake with an appliance doesn't mean adults have suddenly become helpless. It means a person encountered something that appeared broken and reported it through the channel available to them.

The same logic could be applied anywhere. A customer asks an employee where something is instead of searching the entire store. A homeowner calls a mechanic for a noise that turns out to be minor. A business owner hires an accountant because they don't understand tax law. Most people rely on specialists for things outside their expertise. That's normal, not evidence of decline.

What's interesting is that the economics of the story tell a different tale than the conclusion. A duplex purchased for $124k generating roughly $1,925 a month in rent suggests the tenants are providing a significant return on the asset. They're not passive observers in that arrangement; they're the reason the investment performs in the first place.

So if the strongest example of widespread helplessness is someone misdiagnosing a microwave, that's actually a pretty weak case. Human beings have always overlooked obvious things. We notice those moments because they're memorable, not because they're new.

>>62292693
>If I put 70k down on a home and rent it out netting $1200/mo after all expenses thats a 20% return.

$1200/month profit on a SFH is rare. most landlords are targeting 1% rule (gross monthly rent is 1% of property purchase price) with 50% of gross monthly rents going to actual and budgeted overhead.

let's say $250k purchase price with 20% down @ 6.5% for 30 years. let's pretend it will rent for $2500/month. in the cities you showed it probably will not, because people living there are poor as hell. but let's just pretend.

>$1250/mo mortgage
>$150/mo property insurance
>$300/mo property taxes
>$50/mo vacancy budget (~2.5% gross rents or 10 days a year)
>$100/mo maintenance budget (rentoids are tough on properties)

we're already up to $1850/mo in expenses on $2500/mo gross rents, so that's $650/mo profit before taxes. should we assume the rentoids are responsible for mowing the lawn and shoveling sidewalks? that might be a risk, because local laws might pin that responsibility on the landlord even if it's in the lease (i.e., your property collects fines and/or liens for violations.)

>$1200/month profit on a SFH is rare.
Well thats literally what I'm getting on my duplex. I put 140,000 down on a 700,000 duplex my costs all in are just under $3600/mo. Lets add $300/mo for maintenance/vacancy just to make you happy. That still gives me a ~18% return at current rent but the rent will always increase while my mortgage stays fixed. And when its payed off I can either pocket that extra money or take out another loan if rates are low and use it for further investments.
And yes they mow their own fucking lawn its in the rental agreement otherwise I send someone to do it and charge them whatever it costs + an administrative penalty.

BTW I'm not disagreeing that its good to invest in the stock market that is how I make most of my money, all I'm saying is that its good to have multiple diverse sources of income so that your finances are secure no matter what. If you're in your 20's with only a few thousand dollars then yes just keep working and invest as much as you can in high growth stocks to maximize your return but once you get closer to 7 digits you need to start thinking about wealth preservation and tax efficiency not just pure growth.

item.title: Why hasn't cryptocurrency technology been adopted yet? — 18 replies — 31.9/hr
item.content:encoded:
Why hasn't cryptocurrency technology been adopted yet?

Open thread • Replies: 18 • Updated: 2026-05-31 21:34 UTC

OP

Why hasn't cryptocurrency technology been adopted yet? Allow me to explain.
Cryptocurrencies support decentralized networks, which are used for data computation and storage. These networks are cheaper to use than traditional server infrastructure.
If it's cheaper, why don't corporations use them? One, there are regulations preventing adoption, but CLARITY will change this in the US. Two, corporations have invested trillions into building and maintaining server infrastructure. Liquidation is risky from there point of view because they don't understand decentralized networks. A few brave corporations will lead this great transition, and when they are successful, everyone will follow.
You are in the final window of being early. Don't fuck this up.

62295887
>>62295815
Are you aware of how annoying credit card fees are for businesses? Do you know how much it costs to build and maintain a server room? Crypto saves businesses money.

62295975
>>62295598
Do you have any idea how much data is processed in the world? In the big data world, there's this concept of "scale factor". Scale factor 1 is a gigabyte. So scale factor 1000 would be a terabyte. Things don't even start to get interesting until you're looking at scale factor 10000. A fairly modest, multiple-times-a-day job for a single company is not uncommonly something like a terabyte. Huge companies can run jobs in the petabyte range. Every single one of these is using an insanely optimized query engine (Hadoop, Spark, etc) running across hundreds or even thousands of nodes that are optimized down to the hardware level. Why do they do this? I'll give you a hint - it's not speed they care about. They optimize for cost. They want to get everything as cheap as possible. A couple of retarded desktops spread around the world isn't going to be 'cheaper'. It would take you two weeks just to LOAD a petabyte of memory, let alone process it.

It's hilarious how completely clueless you idiots are about what constitutes "compute". The kinds of things you're thinking about are below toy level.

62296016
>>62295975
I'm aware of the scaling. DePIN is scalable to match and eventually exceed the processing and storage capabilities of servers.
Cute post.

62296032
>>62296016
Ridiculous. A group of randomly connected, disparate pieces of hardware will never even come close to the capacity (or usage price) of dedicated, tuned hardware. You're being defrauded with these promises.

62296045
>>62296032
You are an old man, history will bury you...

62296210
>>62296032

>outputs start to get a little blurry 4-5 weeks out and then just turn into noise, which is why I do the updates every 4 weeks

62286447
>>62286347
>Why not update weekly? or even daily?
Basically, because it's a lot easier - there are a lot of inputs into the model that each have to be harvested and processed by me before they get fed into the sim. It runs into the hours each time. I can also cope that the current date's "conditions" are obviously the starting point of the forecast, so having them flattening out a little bit is easier to align
I do weekly updates on the site and the twitter account, but that's 90% just applying current price momentum and FX momentum to the existing physics-based forecast, which I have to get better at. I'm a nerd, not a trader

Women avoid introverted hobbies like the AIDS. Women like money, socializing, displays of psychopathy, extraversion, low IQ, physicality, etc.

62294961
>>62294724
gb2 reddit normalfag

62294979
>>62294947

Man, this argument falls apart the second you stop treating women like a hive mind.

You're basically taking a few traits you've noticed in some people and stretching them across billions of individuals. That's not insight; that's stereotyping with extra steps. There are tons of women who spend their free time reading, gaming, drawing, writing, coding, collecting random niche interests, or disappearing into obscure rabbit holes for hours. The idea that women avoid introverted hobbies "like AIDS" is just obviously false to anyone who's spent five minutes online.

The money and psychopath thing isn't much stronger. If women were only attracted to wealth, status, and flashy antisocial behavior, then shy guys, nerds, artists, academics, and regular dudes would never date. Yet they do, every day. Reality keeps refusing to cooperate with the theory.

And the "normalfags are female-minded" line is weird too. Most people care about relationships because relationships are a major part of human life. That's true for men and women. Having goals outside of niche hobbies doesn't mean someone worships sex. It just means they have different priorities.

The claim that women prefer low IQ is probably the funniest part. In the real world, competence, intelligence, and capability are generally attractive traits. Not universally, because humans are complicated, but far more often than the caricature you're describing.

The biggest problem with this worldview is that it tries to reduce millions of different people into one cynical template. Some women are introverts. Some are extroverts. Some care about money a lot. Some barely care at all. Some like athletes. Some like engineers. Some like artists. Human beings are way too varied to fit into a single edgy slogan.

At some point the theory starts looking less like an observation about women and more like a story you're telling yourself about them.

item.title: Question for oldfags about vidya prices: — 53 replies — 4.8/hr
item.content:encoded:
Question for oldfags about vidya prices:

Open thread • Replies: 53 • Updated: 2026-05-31 11:01 UTC

OP

i looked into the price of SNES games as well as master system back in the days:

-average snes game sold for 50$ in the early 9às, inflation adjusted that's 120$ today
Some sold higher than that : donkey kong came for 70$, so 150$ today

-30 to 60$ for sega master system, but it was the late 80s so like 130$ today.

Why are gooners crying about the price of their vidyas? Things used to be way more expensive for a 2 d platformer that would give you max 100 hours of play if not half. Also today's collector price for most of those games is still LOWER than what people paid for when it came out brand new back in the days, so still no profit after holding for 30+ years kek

Early replies (first 3)

62283131
>>62283119
There was a time not so long ago when you could hardly give that shit away. Nobody wanted to play old 2D games when there was amazing new stuff coming out that you couldn't have even imagined a few years prior.

62283133
>>62283119
Oldfag here, they’re crying because all these games were $1 at your local flea market in 2005, AND THEY DIDN’T EVEN WANT TO BUY. It’s even worse than missing bitcoin because it was right under their nose, they saw, they knew, and they shrugged it off.

62283135
>>62283131

62286669
>>62283133
Nobody is talking about used video games. They're crying because modern consoles are approaching a thousand bucks and moden games are heading towards 100. But anon correctly noted that back in the day, it was $50-60 for a new NES cartridge or a new computer game on three 360kb diskettes, and at the time minimum wage was something like $4 an hour. Adjusted for inflation, a game like Super Mario that you could beat in an hour, or Kings Quest that lasts you a couple of hours max, cost $161.

62287647
>>62283131
>There was a time not so long ago when you could hardly give that shit away. Nobody wanted to play old 2D games when there was amazing new stuff coming out that you couldn't have even imagined a few years prior.

I bought a movie rental stores stock for peanuts when they were going broke in 2012, walked in and offered 2000$ cash for the lot (about 5000 DVDs and blu rays and 2500 PS1, PS2 and 360 games). It was a niche movie buff DVD and game store. Have a pretty complete movie and game library and enjoy it a lot to this day. I am randy marsh.

62287668
>>62286669
>Nobody is talking about used video games.
OP was literally talking about "today's collector price for most of those games" but okay

62287673
>>62287668

62287727
>>62283119

62288182
>>62287668
Yes.
>today's colllector price is still LOWER than what people paid for when it came out brand new
He's still talking about how prices today are still lower when accounting for inflation, even for used collector games, compared to the price for games back in the day.
And he's right. A nintendo entertainment system with one game (Mario/Duck Hunt) adjusted for inflation cost $450.
A New 386 16 with 1mb of ram and a 20mb hard drive with 16 color graphics and midi sound card cost $8500, adjusted for inflation that's $25,000.
People bitching about prices for games these days really have no idea. Their idea of what things cost back in the day is skewed by a combination television where the latest technology was placed "in the common home" as a deliberate advertisement and a long run of (relatively) dramatically discounted prices from the late 90s until the late 10s.
Even today their expectations are ridiculous: "It shouldn't cost as much as when you had physical media because you can just download it!", like servers and connections that can provide fast 85gb video game downloads to millions of people around the world are not expensive themselves.

62288229
>>62283119
People's real wages were also higher.

62288318
>>62283119
I played that game on that console when I was a kid.

62288477
>>62287673
>>62288182
This is true, video games are cheaper in real terms and even in nominal terms today. Even the "expensive" Switch games are not pricier than Street Fighter II in 1993. But the essential things of life are much more expensive (rent, food), which makes video games comparatively just as hard (or harder?) to afford. Hence the complaining.

The crybabies will also complain when a game is free though:
>waaaah I don’t get enough pulls to get my waifu in Genshin Impact, even though I spent $0 in five years of playing daily

62288623
>>62288477
>>62288318
>>62288182
i had a friend at school who had a dedicated playroom room with every fucking super nes game as well as mega drive. Spoiled brat, parents probably were LOADED. That made me diamond at the time

62288647
>>62283119
vidya felt way more expensive and special. buying a new vidya was an important life decision for many, you had to study that shit, read game magazine articles about vidya to weigh which to invest in, you had to save up money, potentially sell other vidya to frens for a bit of cash (like actual paper bills or even actual metal coins). back then when a neighbor kid got a new vidya it was an event and kids would gather to check it out or ask to borrow it someday. i invested all my money in vidya and bought a new one per month. don't get me started on buying a new console, that felt similar to buying a new fridge, something you needed to buy for basic survival because the old one was just not doing it anymore.

62288698
>>62288623
>>62288647
Kek, back then you knew exactly how many games you had, and could recite the list of all your games like it’s a poem.

Each game was considered a rather big purchase and the games felt more "precious" in general. Also true with the PS1-era games.

62288801
>>62283119
muh inflation is a cope shit tier non-argument.
snes and mega drive games were also printed on very expensive cartridges and game makers also had to pay a lot to retail.
now most sales are digital, so there is literally zero actual scarcity or logistics involved and the market is way bigger than it ever was.

basically kys

62289331
>>62288229
>People's real wages were also higher.
No, they really weren't. The people making this claim base it on the price of Gold in 1970 and extrapolate it from there. (or they compare themselves to characters on 70s and 80s television) The price of Gold was artificially suppressed for 30 years by Bretton Woods. The median salary in 1987 was $30,000 a year. If you made above 60,000 you were upper middle class. Pic related was a middle class home and cost 120k new. People also like to pretend that there weren't oil shocks and 20% inflation in the 70s and 80s wiping out that majority of boomers' savings, or that a mortgage for such a home didn't come with a 16% interest rate.

62289351
>>62288801
>there is literally zero actual scarcity or logistics involved
Servers and high speed connections are not zero logistics.
You don't even put two and two together. In the heady days of the wild west internet of the early 2000s, bandwidth was cheap, but it was also slow. People don't realize that because they demanded HD (and later 4K) streaming that infrastructure costs have skyrocketed so much that only the richest corporations can afford to provide that kind of service. You wonder why there's so much consolidation of media, why the major corporations have bought up so many smaller studios, why social media and video streaming is reduced to a handful of winners, it's because the bandwidth necessary to transmit the large amounts of data you're demanding is huge, and Bandwidth ain't actually cheap at all.

62289407
>>62283119
games used to actually be good, and they provided you with hundreds of hours of entertainment. not the dog shit coming out today that you beat in 8 hrs and then never play again

62289601
>>62283119
This game took me 4 days to beat as a kid so definitely not 100 hours.

62289722
>>62289351
>Servers and high speed connections are not zero logistics.
retarded.

62290884
>>62283119

62290896
>>62283119
>inflation adjusted that's 120$ today
wages arent inflation adjusted.

62291071
>>62286669
>Kings Quest that lasts you a couple of hours max
Kings Quest actually had some difficult puzzles. Nobody beat it in a couple hours without cheats and/or walkthroughs

62291136
>>62283119
Daily good were relatively cheaper, so it evened out or felt less bad

62291154
>muh inflation
>>62289351
The developer would have gotten 7 dollars off of a 70 dollar 4MB SNES cartridge. Seven.
Today they get around 20. Adjust that for inflation and they've beaten inflation. About 15 bucks if you take cpi is what the cut in the snes era was worth.

Everything about digital distribution is cheaper than sticking roms into plastic boxes and shipping them internationally for them to sit in warehouses and later retail stores. And it could be even cheaper Steam's monopoly keeps things artificially high.

62291182
>>62291154
>Steam
I've been buying off GOG lately, unfortunately they don't have everything.

62292787
>>6228313
it's cause we literally moved on to zoomer older zoomer and very young millenial nostalgia. It's kind of crazy wathing people froth with nostalgia over the SNES only to forget it followed by frothing at even mid games on the N64. And now things are so warped people think gamecube is the best Nintendo console.

62292811
You could get new Gamecubes and PS2's for like $60-$100 MSRP a few years after launch. Controllers were $20. A Switch 2 is $500+ with $90+ controllers, and the games are way worse. You are full of shit and a retard, OP.

62294347
>>62283119
My brother sold my Earthbound game that I bought when it came out for like $600 a few years back. The whole thing was a sting operation and the buyer was really a cop who then checked it to see if it was real. I think my brother got to keep the $600 and the cops took the real game.

62294480
>>62292811
nintendo is failling horribly, but this is beyond the point. the PS5 is more or less the same price inflation adjusted than the super nes or master system when it came out and its game are cheaper
Ure the tard sir

62294592
>>62283119

Well keep in mind there was only two consoles worth giving a fuck about.. most of us or at least the people i knew we all rented games from the video shop.. we were born into our parents going to the video shops and renting us videos to watch, so naturally that extended to gaming. For a couple dollars i played donkey kong for about 3 days when it came out. Owning games were like getting only 1-2 games per year on birthdays and xmas , we also shared a lot of our games with friends. There wasnt many good games to choose from so if you had mario and something else you were golden for the long haul. We got our main gaming fix from arcade centers, they were big back in those days ~ sega nintendo were still very inferior to what arcades provided, they were what it seemed generations behind of their time up until the ps1 came out that started the change, then cheaper computers and faster internet = death of arcades + video shops

tldr: we did more with less
